title: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The process of introducing a product so novel that it defines an entirely new market segment rather than competing within an existing one.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An individual within an organization who actively promotes and advocates for a new idea, technology, or product concept, often without formal authority or guaranteed reward.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The process by which ideas, technologies, or insights from one domain or department are transferred to another, often producing novel applications.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A cognitive strategy in which a product characteristic initially perceived as a flaw is reconceptualized as a benefit or core value proposition in a different context.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An innovation that creates a fundamentally new product category or radically changes how a need is met, as opposed to incremental improvements on existing products.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A sudden, often emotionally vivid insight in which a solution to a problem becomes clear.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The practice of promoting an idea or technology within an organization to build support, resources, and adoption, particularly when the idea lacks obvious commercial precedent.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A consumer need that is real and experienced but has not been consciously articulated or connected to a product solution.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: Tiny spherical acrylic polymer beads developed by Spencer Silver in 1968 that form the core of the Post-it Note's adhesive, enabling contact stickiness without permanent bonding and clean repositioning.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: The phase of new product development in which a firm recognizes an unmet or underserved need that could be addressed by a new product or service.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An adhesive that allows a bonded surface to be peeled away and reattached multiple times without losing tack or leaving residue.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An unexpected finding that arises from research aimed at a different goal, which nonetheless proves valuable. Requires researchers to remain observant when results diverge from expectations.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: An innovation model in which a new technology is developed first and a market application is identified afterward, as opposed to demand-pull where a known customer need drives R&D.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: Proprietary business information — including formulas, processes, or methods — that a company protects by keeping confidential rather than by filing a patent.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History

cards.definition: A pattern of product diffusion in which users naturally expose non-users to the product through ordinary use, triggering organic demand without paid advertising.
cards.sourceLecture: Supplementary Reading: Post-It Note Innovation History
